    <h1 {...{
      "id": "understanding-uxp-apis"
    }}>{`Understanding UXP APIs`}</h1>
    <p>{`Learn about the two types of APIs available in UXP and when to use each one`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "overview"
    }}>{`Overview`}</h2>
    <p>{`Now that you have successfully built your first `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../plugins/index.md"
      }}>{`plugin`}</a>{`, you're ready to tackle more complex tasks. The key to building useful plugins is understanding which APIs to use and when.`}</p>
    <p>{`The UXP platform provides two complementary sets of APIs:`}</p>
    <ol>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`UXP Core APIs`}</strong>{`: for building user interfaces, file operations, network calls, and general functionality.`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol"><strong parentName="li">{`Premiere APIs`}</strong>{`: for interacting with and modifying Premiere projects and sequences.`}</li>
    </ol>
    <p>{`Most often than not, you will need to use both APIs together to create the functionality you want. Let's explore each one and see how they work.`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "uxp-core-apis"
    }}>{`UXP Core APIs`}</h2>
    <p>{`UXP Core APIs provide the fundamental building blocks for your plugin's functionality, and are `}<strong parentName="p">{`shared`}</strong>{` across all Adobe Creative Cloud applications that support UXP. These APIs let you:`}</p>
    <ul>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Create user interfaces`}</strong>{` using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Access the file system`}</strong>{` to read and write files`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Make network requests`}</strong>{` to communicate with external services`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Handle clipboard operations`}</strong>{` for copy/paste functionality`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Work with system utilities`}</strong>{` like shell commands and OS information`}</li>
    </ul>
    <h3 {...{
      "id": "accessing-uxp-apis"
    }}>{`Accessing UXP APIs`}</h3>
    <p>{`The way you access UXP APIs depends on the specific API itself.`}</p>
    <p><a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../uxp-api/reference-js/Global%20Members/index.md"
      }}><strong parentName="a">{`Global APIs`}</strong></a>{` are available immediately without any import. For example:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`// Crypto API is globally available
const hash = crypto.randomUUID();

// Document API is globally available
const button = document.createElement("sp-button");
`}</code></pre>
    <p><a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../uxp-api/reference-js/Modules/index.md"
      }}><strong parentName="a">{`Module-based APIs`}</strong></a>{` require importing with `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`require()`}</inlineCode>{`. For example:`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`// Parent UXP module
const uxp = require("uxp");

// File system access
const fs = require("fs");

// Operating system utilities
const os = require("os");
`}</code></pre>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Permission-based APIs`}</strong>{` also need to be allow-listed in your plugin's `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`manifest.json`}</inlineCode>{`. For example, to use the `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../plugins/concepts/manifest/index.md#localfilesystem"
      }}>{`file system`}</a>{` or make `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../plugins/concepts/manifest/index.md#network"
      }}>{`network requests`}</a>{`, you must declare the appropriate permissions. Learn more about permissions in the `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../plugins/concepts/manifest/index.md#requiredpermissions"
      }}>{`Manifest guide`}</a>{`.`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "premiere-apis"
    }}>{`Premiere APIs`}</h2>
    <p>{`Premiere APIs, also interchangeably called `}<strong parentName="p">{`DOM APIs`}</strong>{` (Document Object Model) or `}<strong parentName="p">{`Premiere DOM APIs`}</strong>{`, give you direct access to Premiere's document model. These APIs let you, among other things, to:`}</p>
    <ul>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Access and modify projects`}</strong>{` — sequences, tracks, clips, and markers`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Work with media`}</strong>{` — import files, manage project items`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Control playback`}</strong>{` — set in/out points, control the playhead`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Apply effects`}</strong>{` — add video and audio effects to clips`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Export content`}</strong>{` — configure and trigger exports`}</li>
    </ul>
    <p>{`The entry point to Premiere APIs is the `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`app`}</inlineCode>{` object, that you `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`require()`}</inlineCode>{` from the `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`"premierepro"`}</inlineCode>{` module.`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`const app = require("premierepro");
`}</code></pre>
    <p>{`You'll learn how to access and use Premiere APIs in the `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../dom-apis/index.md"
      }}>{`Working with Premiere APIs`}</a>{` section and by browsing the `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../ppro_reference/index.md"
      }}>{`Premiere API reference`}</a>{`.`}</p>
    <InlineAlert variant="warning" slots="header, text" mdxType="InlineAlert" />
    <p>{`Don't confuse the two DOMs`}</p>
    <p>{`The `}<strong parentName="p">{`Premiere DOM`}</strong>{` controls the Premiere document (projects, sequences, clips). The `}<strong parentName="p">{`HTML DOM`}</strong>{` controls your plugin's user interface (buttons, inputs, panels). They are completely separate systems.`}</p>
    <h3 {...{
      "id": "unified-javascript-engine"
    }}>{`Unified JavaScript Engine`}</h3>
    <p>{`As the platform's name `}<a parentName="p" {...{
        "href": "../../../index.md"
      }}>{`suggests`}</a>{`, UXP provides a `}<em parentName="p">{`unified`}</em>{` JavaScript engine that has `}<strong parentName="p">{`direct access to both the Premiere APIs and the UXP Core APIs`}</strong>{`. This is a big advantage over the previous extensibility technology (CEP), where the communication between the extension logic and the host application happened through a bridge (CSInterface) that passed messages back and forth between the two runtimes.`}</p>
    <p>{`With UXP, everything runs natively in the same environment, and you just need to `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`require()`}</inlineCode>{` the appropriate modules to access the APIs you need.`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "practical-example"
    }}>{`Practical Example`}</h2>
    <p>{`Let's see how both API types work together. Imagine you want to build a plugin that:`}</p>
    <ol>
      <li parentName="ol">{`Reads text content from a file on the user's computer`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol">{`Creates a new text layer in the active sequence`}</li>
      <li parentName="ol">{`Applies a specific style to the text layer`}</li>
    </ol>
    <p>{`Here's which APIs you would use for each step:`}</p>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Step 1: Read file content`}</strong>{` — Use UXP's `}<inlineCode parentName="p">{`fs`}</inlineCode>{` module`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`const fs = require("fs");
const file = await fs.getFileForOpening();
const content = await file.read();
`}</code></pre>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Step 2: Create text layer`}</strong>{` — Use Premiere APIs`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`const app = require("premierepro");
const sequence = app.project.activeSequence;
// Add text layer to sequence
`}</code></pre>
    <p><strong parentName="p">{`Step 3: Apply styling`}</strong>{` — Use both APIs`}</p>
    <pre><code parentName="pre" {...{
        "className": "language-javascript"
      }}>{`// Premiere APIs to modify the clip properties
// UXP APIs to show progress in your plugin's UI
`}</code></pre>
    <p>{`This demonstrates how UXP Core APIs handle the infrastructure, while Premiere APIs handle the creative content manipulation.`}</p>
    <h2 {...{
      "id": "api-versioning-and-compatibility"
    }}>{`API Versioning and Compatibility`}</h2>
    <p>{`Both UXP and Premiere APIs are actively evolving, with new capabilities added in each release. It's important to understand how versioning works to ensure your plugin functions correctly.`}</p>
    <h3 {...{
      "id": "host-application-and-uxp"
    }}>{`Host Application and UXP`}</h3>
    <ul>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`Premiere version`}</strong>{` (v25.6): determines which Premiere DOM APIs are available.`}</li>
      <li parentName="ul"><strong parentName="li">{`UXP version`}</strong>{` (v8.1): determines which UXP Core APIs are available.`}</li>
    </ul>
    <p>{`Each Premiere release integrates a specific UXP version. For example, Premiere v25.6 includes UXP v8.1.`}</p>
    <InlineAlert variant="info" slots="header, text, text2" mdxType="InlineAlert" />
    <p>{`Handling Version Mismatches`}</p>
    <p>{`If your plugin relies on an API introduced in UXP v8.2, but the user is running Premiere v25.6 (which only includes UXP v8.1), it will throw errors and fail to run. The same applies if your plugin depends on APIs added in Premiere v25.7 but the user is still on v25.6.`}</p>
    <p>{`To avoid compatibility issues, make sure to target APIs common to both versions, or implement fallback logic for older releases when possible.`}</p>
    <h3 {...{
      "id": "checking-versions"
    }}>{`Checking Versions`}</h3>
    <p>{`You can determine the UXP version in several ways:`}</p>

      "id": "1-in-the-uxp-developer-tool"
    }}>{`1. In the UXP Developer Tool`}</h4>
